claim: Buffer adopted a permanent 4-day workweek (32 hours, full pay) in 2021 and as of July 2025 had not reverted; CEO Joel Gascoigne reported record-high ARR and profitability under the policy.

Buffer's 2022 internal survey found 91% of employees reported being happier and more productive under the 4-day week, but 16% said they could NOT complete all work in 4 days, and some employees reported feeling disconnected from colleagues due to reduced meeting time.

Alter Agents, a Los Angeles market research firm, abandoned its 10-week 4-day workweek trial after employee satisfaction declined; staff struggled to set boundaries and worked on their day off, and the team voted to end the experiment.
Bolt, a $14 billion US fintech, quietly removed its 4-day workweek policy in 2024 after CEO Ryan Breslow had previously declared the company was 'never going back' to a 5-day week.

Power Within Training, a UK client-facing consultancy serving construction industry leaders, ended its 4-day workweek trial because leads went unanswered from Thursday afternoon to Monday, causing client loss to 5-day competitors.

Thrive, a UK e-learning company, abandoned its 4-day week because client-facing teams could not take the day off while clients worked 5 days; internal tension grew between creative teams who loved the policy and sales teams who felt pressure to stay always-on.
source: Cited via Fast Company and CFIB articles (see CFIB-FCEI.ca tools-resources/4-day-work-week)

claim: Krystal, a London-based tech company, canceled its 4-day week trial after 4 months (it was supposed to be 6 weeks) due to increased stress among staff; the founder said the extra recovery time did not compensate for the 20% increase in output required.

Allcap, a Gloucester engineering and industrial supplies firm, withdrew from the UK 4-day week trial 2 months early at its main trade sites because employees experienced 'nine intense workdays' in a cycle instead of a genuine compressed schedule.
source: UK 4 Day Week Global pilot reporting; cited in multiple news outlets

claim: Bell Canada moved 12,000 technicians to a 4-day week (four 9-hour days) in 1994 but abandoned it after 1 year due to scheduling mismatches (Mondays/Fridays off on busiest days), low manager buy-in, unproductive 9th hours, and dramatically increased overtime costs.

Morrisons, a UK supermarket chain, scrapped its 4-day workweek trial for head office staff in 2020 after employees were required to work one Saturday every four weeks, undermining the work-life balance the policy was meant to provide.
Treehouse, a US online learning platform, reverted to a 5-day week in September 2016 after laying off 22 employees; the CEO felt it was inappropriate to maintain the 4-day policy amid job cuts.
source: Original Treehouse blog; cited in startups.co.uk four-day-work-week-trials/

claim: Launceston City Council in Tasmania, Australia, proposed a 4-day, 30.4-hour week with no pay cut in January 2026 but shelved the plan one month later following intense criticism; documents revealed no standalone business case existed for the proposal.
Asda, a UK supermarket chain, scrapped its compressed 4-day workweek after employees complained that longer shifts were 'physically demanding' and left them 'exhausted.'

Unilever called the 4-day workweek model 'rigid' and stated 'there is no one-size-fits-all approach to flexibility' when declining to adopt it company-wide. Bupa similarly said a 'one-size-fits-all approach will not suit our workforce.'

A 2025 study of IKEA Belgium's compressed schedule, published in BMC Public Health, found that while workers experienced increased psychological detachment, this did NOT translate into reduced work-related exhaustion or burnout risk.
source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12889-025-21323-4 ('Time Tetris: a longitudinal study on compressed schedules and workplace well-being at IKEA', 2025)

claim: Working 12 hours per day is associated with a 37% increased risk of injury compared with 8-hour shifts; accident risk in the 10th hour of work is 90% higher than in the first 8 hours.

The UK 4-day week trial (2022, 61 organizations) was not peer-reviewed, was coordinated by advocacy group 4 Day Week Global, had 9 of 70 initial companies drop out before starting, achieved only a 7% reduction in days worked (4.86 to 4.52) not the advertised 20%, and relied on self-reported data with ~58% survey response rate.

The Iceland trials (2015-2019, 2,500 public-sector workers) were widely misreported as '4-day week' successes; the actual reduction was only 1-3 hours per week (to 35-36 hours), and 61 of 66 workplaces reduced by only 1-3 hours. Final negotiated agreements resulted in just 35 minutes less per week in the private sector and 65 minutes in the public sector.

The European Commission Joint Research Centre (2023) identified multiple methodological flaws in 4-day week pilot studies including: no pre-registration or pre-analysis plans, no balance checks between treatment and comparison groups, multiple outcomes analyzed without adjusting for multiple comparisons, and survey response rates as low as 50%.
When Japan shortened its workweek from 46 to 30 hours in 1988, productivity did not increase enough to compensate, and economic output was 20% lower over the following eight years.

Research led by Juliet Schor (Boston College) for 4 Day Week Global across 245 businesses and nonprofits found that approximately 8% (20 companies) returned to a 5-day schedule one year after their pilot.
source: 4 Day Week Global research cited via FOX 10 and SIOP reports

claim: According to MIT Sloan Management Review, work does not disappear under a 4-day week; it 'compresses into longer individual days, spills into the fifth day unofficially, or accumulates into a backlog that erodes productivity gains.' The review notes that 'for every success story, there is a quieter failure.'

Client-facing businesses (consulting, agencies, law firms, market research) face structural challenges with 4-day weeks: their clients and suppliers operate on 5-day schedules, creating coverage gaps, response delays, and competitive disadvantages that several companies (Thrive, Power Within Training, Alter Agents, KLA) cited as reasons for reverting.
source: Multiple company case studies aggregated from Fast Company, Forbes, and Autonomy research
